verses.text: Balaam saw it pleased the LORD to bless Israel, and went not as other times to seek enchantments. He lifted up his eyes — saw Israel abiding in their tents according to their tribes; the Spirit of God came upon him. Guidance: He stops trying to twist God's arm. When he finally just LOOKS at Israel as they are — tents in tribal order under the open sky — the Spirit falls. Sometimes the prophet's task is simply to see what God has already done.

Balaam saw it pleased the LORD to bless Israel, and went not as other times to seek enchantments. He lifted up his eyes — saw Israel abiding in their tents according to their tribes; the Spirit of God came upon him. Guidance: He stops trying to twist God's arm. When he finally just LOOKS at Israel as they are — tents in tribal order under the open sky — the Spirit falls. Sometimes the prophet's task is simply to see what God has already done.

The man whose eyes are open hath said: how goodly are thy tents, O Jacob, thy tabernacles, O Israel! As valleys spread forth, gardens by the river, cedars beside waters. His king higher than Agag. He couched as a lion — who shall stir him up? Blessed is he that blesseth thee, cursed is he that curseth thee. Guidance: The man with open eyes sees what hatred could never see. Tents become gardens, a refugee camp becomes a kingdom. The Abraham promise echoes — bless him and be blessed, curse him and be cursed. Balaam knows this now from his own forced mouth.

Balak's anger kindled, he smote his hands together: I called thee to curse, thou hast blessed altogether three times. Flee to thy place. Balaam: if Balak give me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot go beyond the LORD. I will tell thee what this people shall do to thy people in the latter days. Guidance: Balak claps in fury; Balaam stands firm — but for the wrong reason. He cannot disobey, but his heart was always trying to. Still, before he leaves, God uses his lips one more time — the longest prophecy is yet to come.

He that heard the words of God: I shall see Him, but not now; I shall behold Him, but not nigh. There shall come a Star out of Jacob, a Sceptre shall rise out of Israel — shall smite the corners of Moab. Edom shall be a possession; out of Jacob shall come he that shall have dominion. Guidance: From the curser's mouth, the Messiah. Not now, not nigh — but a Star is coming. Centuries later magi from Balaam's region will see one rising over a stable. The prophecy God forced from a wicked prophet found its way home.

Balaam looked on Amalek: his latter end shall be that he perish for ever. On Kenites: thou puttest thy nest in a rock — yet shalt be wasted. Ships shall come from Chittim, afflict Asshur, afflict Eber. Balaam rose, returned to his place, Balak also went his way. Guidance: A sweep through history from his perch: empires rise on rocks and fall in the sea. Then the man walks home. Balaam saw the future of nations — but missed his own. The eye that sees God's plan must learn to read the prophet's own. Numbers 31 will tell us what Balaam did with his return ticket.
